report: The patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive with a blank stare. There is no visible movement or signs of automatisms, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. The patient exhibits right arm flexion and slight head version to the right, and remains in a resting position throughout the observed period.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion and extension. The movements appear unilateral without spreading to other body parts.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion and slight head version to the right. The movements progress to involve more pronounced arm flexion, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head tilt and subtle arm movements. The patient remains unresponsive with minimal observable motor activity.

The patient is lying in bed, initially focused on a laptop. As the video progresses, the patient lifts and adjusts the laptop's position, pointing at the screen and demonstrating continuous interaction with the device. There is no observable seizure activity, including blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks.

The patient initially appears to be resting, then exhibits a blank stare followed by slight head movement. There is no significant motor activity or spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements observed.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head turn to the right. No significant motor signs such as arm flexion, extension, or clonic jerks are observed, and there is no spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the video.

The patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive with minimal or no movement throughout the video. A blank stare is observed initially, accompanied by subtle head movement and slight arm flexion in one instance; however, there are no other signs of seizures such as lip smacking, head version, or repetitive arm movements. The patient remains unresponsive and largely still, with no significant change in position or observable activity during the observation period.

The patient initially appears unresponsive with a blank stare, followed by a slight head turn to the left and subtle left arm flexion. The movements are minimal and localized without progression to more pronounced tonic or clonic activity.

The patient is lying still in bed, appearing unresponsive with eyes open and no visible signs of seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. No observable movements or automatisms are present, and the patient remains still throughout the observation. At one point, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by head version to the left with left arm flexion. The seizure progresses to involve bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with rhythmic jerking of the limbs. In another episode, the patient shows a blank stare followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension, which also progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerking of both arms. The patient remains unresponsive during all seizure episodes.

The patient is lying in a hospital bed, initially appearing unresponsive with eyes closed and a blank stare. Seizure activity begins with lateralizing features: one episode starts with left arm flexion and right arm extension, another with right arm flexion and left arm extension, and a third with rightward head version and right arm flexion. These episodes progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed events and shows no other signs of seizure activity such as lip smacking. During periods without seizure activity, the patient remains still and motionless.

The patient initially appears to be in a resting position and exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version—upward and to the left—and bilateral arm flexion with left arm predominance. This progresses to tonic stiffening and bilateral clonic movements, including rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs, accompanied by backward tilting of the head and elevation of the arms in a clonic manner. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode, consistent with a bilateral tonic-clonic seizure.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by lip smacking and head version to the right with right arm extension. The seizure progresses in some episodes to include right arm flexion and left arm extension, as well as bilateral arm involvement with rhythmic movements showing alternating flexion and extension, suggesting spread to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. Clonic jerks and bilateral arm flexion are observed during progression, with the patient becoming unresponsive throughout the episodes. In certain instances, there is no visible spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements or rhythmic jerking.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by lateralized motor signs including left arm flexion with right arm extension or head version to the left with left arm flexion. The seizure may progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerking and unresponsiveness. In some episodes, right arm flexion and left arm extension are observed, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout.

The patient initially appears to be resting, then exhibits a blank stare followed by slight head movement. The patient turns to the left side, partially lifting the head and arm, and remains in this position. In subsequent events, the patient consistently exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm flexion, with progression to left arm extension and right arm extension resembling fencer posturing in one instance. Rhythmic jerks ensue during one of these episodes. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with minimal movement observed in some cases.

The patient is lying on a hospital bed, initially appearing unresponsive with their head slightly tilted back and no visible movement or signs of seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, or limb flexion/extension. In some episodes, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by variable motor manifestations: one episode shows a slight head turn to the left without significant movement or spread; another shows leftward head version with left arm flexion progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening and clonic jerks involving both arms; two separate episodes involve right arm flexion and left arm extension. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed events. In contrast, one observation notes the patient initially lying still, then becoming alert and responsive, shifting position, raising an arm, and sitting up in bed.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion and slight head version to the right. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the observed period.

The patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive with a blank stare. There is no visible movement, change in position, or signs of tonic-clonic activity, automatisms, or rhythmic jerks, and the patient remains in a fixed position throughout the video. At times, the head is tilted backward with the mouth open. The right arm is slightly flexed, and the left arm is positioned across the chest. Episodes include initial blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with slight subsequent arm movements; however, there is no significant spread to bilateral movements or tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or generalized convulsive activity observed.

The patient is seated on a hospital bed, at times holding a remote control and appearing focused or interacting calmly with staff, while at other times exhibiting episodes characterized by a blank stare followed by right arm flexion and rightward head version, during which the patient remains unresponsive. During responsive periods, the patient appears alert, relaxed, and cooperative, with no visible seizure activity such as lip smacking, automatisms, tonic-clonic movements, rhythmic jerks, or unilateral/bilateral stiffening. The arms remain resting on the lap or engaged in purposeful activity, and the patient adjusts position occasionally, including crossing legs and shifting slightly. Following seizure-like episodes, the patient appears to enter a postictal state marked by unresponsiveness.

The patient exhibits a blank stare at the onset of each episode, followed by asymmetric motor manifestations. Initial signs include right arm flexion with left arm extension or right arm extension with left arm flexion, the latter resembling fencer posturing. Head version to the left is observed in some episodes, accompanied by left arm flexion and right arm extension. Tonic stiffening and clonic jerks follow the initial focal features, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements in some cases, while in others, there is no secondary generalization. The patient remains unresponsive during all observed events.

The patient exhibits episodes characterized by a blank stare, often accompanied by unresponsiveness. In some instances, the blank stare is followed by a slight head turn to the left, isolated left arm movement toward the head, or asymmetric motor activity such as right arm flexion with left arm extension. Bilateral arm involvement is observed in other episodes, including extension and flexion with rhythmic jerking movements above the head. At other times, the patient remains unresponsive with no observable seizure activity—such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks—and shows minimal or no movement. This includes periods with eyes closed, open mouth, or lying still on the bed without any significant motor activity. No spread to generalized bilateral tonic-clonic activity is noted across the observation period.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version to the left and associated left arm flexion. In some instances, this progresses to unilateral arm flexion with slight clonic jerks or tonic stiffening and clonic jerking, remaining unresponsive throughout. At times, the seizure activity involves right arm extension with left arm flexion, accompanied by erratic arm movements and signs of distress. The patient is observed in varying positions: initially lying on their side, then turning onto their back with bilateral arm elevation, extension, and rhythmic clonic movements. No consistent spread to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures or automatisms is observed across all episodes. A medical professional intervenes during one episode to secure an oxygen mask.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by motor manifestations that include left arm flexion with head turning to the left and slight extension of the right arm, progressing to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerking involving both arms. In multiple instances, the patient displays right arm extension and left arm flexion in a fencer-like posturing. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed events.

The patient is lying on a hospital bed, appearing unresponsive throughout the observation. The head is turned to the left, and the arms assume asymmetric positioning: the right arm is extended along the bed or bed rail, while the left arm is flexed, either bent at the elbow with the hand near the head or holding onto the bed rail. This posture resembles fencer posturing. The episode begins with a blank stare followed by right arm flexion and extension, which progresses to involve both arms—right arm extending and left arm flexing—before settling into a static, motionless state. There are no further visible signs of seizure activity such as tonic stiffening or clonic jerks, and the patient remains unresponsive and without movement for the duration of the video.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension. The patient then transitions to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs and remains unresponsive throughout the event. At times, the patient is observed lying on their side with their head turned toward their bent arm, which rests near their face, showing no movement or change in position during these periods.

The patient initially appears unresponsive with a blank stare and slight head turn (version) to one side, either left or right. This is followed by unilateral arm flexion—either in the left or right arm—accompanied by subtle rhythmic jerks. In some instances, the head rotation and arm movement are ipsilateral. The movements remain focal and do not progress to bilateral tonic-clonic activity or automatisms. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the observation, with no further seizure evolution noted.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm flexion and slight head turning or version to the right. The patient shifts position at times, pulling the blanket over themselves, then transitions to a more relaxed posture with arms resting on the abdomen. Throughout all episodes, the patient remains unresponsive.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm extension and slight left arm flexion. The patient then transitions to a more relaxed posture with arms resting by the sides, remaining unresponsive throughout.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by right arm extension and slight head version to the right. The patient exhibits a blank stare at the onset of each episode, followed by various motor manifestations. These include rhythmic arm movements with bilateral flexion and extension, progressing in some instances to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with coordinated arm movements and generalized body stiffening. The patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive with eyes closed and hands clasped together, showing no observable movement or signs of seizure activity in some segments. In multiple instances, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by various motor manifestations. These include lip smacking without progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements or significant automatisms. Other observations note rhythmic head movements and arm flexion, either remaining focal or spreading to involve both arms with clonic jerks. Rightward head version accompanied by right arm flexion is noted in several segments, without spread to bilateral tonic-clonic activity or other automatisms, and the patient remains unresponsive throughout these episodes. One segment describes initial right arm and head movement followed by purposeful adjustment of the pillow, indicating a lack of tonic-clonic activity or rhythmic jerking. Asynchronous rhythmic jerking of both arms with occasional left head turning is observed in one case. In another instance, the blank stare is followed by right arm extension and left arm flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of arms and legs. Across all episodes where altered responsiveness occurs, the patient remains unresponsive during the events.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rhythmic head movements and arm flexion, which in some instances progress to involve both arms with clonic jerks and slight torso involvement. In several observations, the activity spreads to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by synchronous jerking of both arms and legs, tonic stiffening, and backward head tilt, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout. Some episodes are localized, involving only the right arm and leg with clonic jerks, or presenting as right arm flexion with head version to the right, without spread to other body parts. One episode includes right arm flexion and left arm extension, remaining focal. In one case, after a blank stare, the patient raises the right arm and places it on the forehead, suggesting possible discomfort or confusion, though no clear seizure activity is otherwise observed. Multiple observations note the patient lying unresponsive with a blank stare but without any motor manifestations, automatisms, or postural changes. Several recordings show no signs of seizure activity—no blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks—and the patient remains still with eyes closed or head tilted back.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version to the right with right arm extension, or head version to the left with left arm flexion or slight arm flexion. In some episodes, this is accompanied by rhythmic head movements, rhythmic jaw movements, or slight head tilting, without visible spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, rhythmic jerks, or other automatisms. The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by lip smacking and rightward head version in multiple episodes, with no visible spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, rhythmic jerks, or other automatisms. In one instance, the blank stare is followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension, during which the patient remains unresponsive. Another episode involves rhythmic head and arm movements, with the right arm flexed and the left arm extended; these movements progress to more pronounced head versioning and arm flexion, indicating a focal seizure with possible secondary generalization. At another time, the patient displays a blank stare with lip smacking and only slight head movement, without progression to tonic-clonic activity or additional automatisms. During one observation period, the patient is lying on a hospital bed, appearing unresponsive with a nasal cannula in place, and shows no observable seizure activity—including no blank stare, lip smacking, head version, or arm flexion/extension—and remains still throughout.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by various motor manifestations, remaining unresponsive throughout each event. Observed patterns include: (1) right arm flexion with left arm extension, resembling fencer posturing; (2) head version to the left with concurrent left arm flexion and right arm extension; (3) rightward head version with right arm extension; and (4) a sudden head tilt backward and upward with an open mouth, suggestive of a possible tonic phase, without visible limb involvement or progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, as well as leftward head version and left arm flexion. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episodes, with no further spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements observed.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by various motor manifestations including right arm flexion, right arm extension, left arm flexion, leftward and rightward head version, and combinations thereof. Several episodes feature a fencer-like posture with head version to one side accompanied by ipsilateral arm flexion and contralateral arm extension, most commonly to the left side. Some events progress from focal onset to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, characterized by tonic stiffening of limbs followed by clonic jerks that spread bilaterally, with rhythmic jerking observed in multiple instances. Other presentations include isolated tonic stiffening of unilateral or bilateral limbs without progression to generalized convulsions, as well as episodes with rhythmic head movements and arm flexion that evolve into bilateral clonic activity. One episode involves a blank stare with retrocollis (head tilted backward) and an open mouth, without limb involvement. During all active events, the patient remains unresponsive. In contrast, multiple segments show the patient lying motionless on a bed, often restrained and connected to medical equipment such as EEG leads, nasal cannula, or breathing mask, with no observable seizure activity—including absence of blank stare, lip smacking, head version, or limb movements—and remaining unresponsive and still throughout these periods.

The patient is lying in bed, appearing unresponsive throughout the video, with no observable movement or change in posture. The head is tilted back with eyes closed in most observations, while some note a blank stare. There is no visible evidence of seizure activity, including absence of tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, automatisms (such as lip smacking), head version, or limb flexion/extension. In one segment, the patient is observed holding a pen in their right hand but remains otherwise static and unresponsive. No signs of seizure or motor activity are present across all observations.

The patient is lying or seated in a hospital bed, appearing unresponsive with a blank stare, eyes closed, or head tilted back. In some segments, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by various motor manifestations including lip smacking, rhythmic head movements, head version (to the left or right), and subtle or rhythmic arm flexion/extension, primarily unilateral and involving either the right or left arm. No tonic stiffening, clonic jerks (except for isolated rhythmic clonic jerks in the right arm in one instance), automatisms beyond lip smacking, or spread of seizure activity to other body regions is observed. In multiple segments, there is no visible movement, change in position, or signs of seizure activity throughout the video, with the patient remaining in a static, still state. The patient remains unresponsive to external stimuli during all observed episodes.

The patient initially appears to be resting or engaged in routine activity (such as eating), then suddenly stops and exhibits a blank stare, indicating the onset of an episode. Following the events, the patient regains awareness, adjusts position, sits up, and reaches for a cup, indicating a return to consciousness and motor control.
